title: Understanding Dog Excitement Urination: Tips and Triage Advice
meta_desc: Learn why dogs leak when excited, the urgency level for this behavior, and how to help your puppy manage their bladder during greetings.
content_html:
What is Excitement Urination?

Excitement urination is an involuntary physical response where a dog, most commonly a puppy, loses bladder control during high-arousal moments. This typically happens during greetings, intense play, or when someone enters the home. Unlike a potty training lapse, the dog isn't choosing to go; their nervous system simply overrides their bladder control temporarily.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for excitement urination is Low. In most cases, this is a behavioral or developmental stage that puppies eventually outgrow. However, if you notice your dog straining to urinate, see blood in the urine, or if the leaking happens while they are sleeping, you should schedule a non-emergency appointment with your veterinarian to rule out medical issues like infections or ectopic ureters.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Providing your vet with a photo of the urine on a light-colored surface can be very helpful. It allows the clinical team to assess the color and concentration of the urine. Additionally, a video of the behavior occurring can help a veterinarian or behaviorist distinguish between excitement urination and submissive urination, which require slightly different management strategies.

faqs.question: Will my puppy grow out of excitement urination?
faqs.answer: Yes, most puppies grow out of this behavior by the time they are one year old as they develop better physical bladder control and emotional maturity.

faqs.question: How should I react when my dog leaks?
faqs.answer: It is important not to punish or scold your dog, as this can lead to anxiety and worsen the problem. Simply clean it up without a fuss and try to keep future greetings very calm.

faqs.question: Can medication help with this behavior?
faqs.answer: While usually managed through behavior modification, in some chronic adult cases, veterinarians may prescribe medications to increase urethral sphincter tone if a medical cause is contributing.
